Today we're going to talk about some really exciting developments in making large language models more efficient. Over the past year, we've seen a convergence of techniques that are fundamentally changing how we deploy these models in production.

Let's start with quantization. The key insight is that you don't need full 32-bit floating point precision for inference. Teams at several labs have shown that 4-bit quantization, particularly using the GPTQ and AWQ methods, can preserve over 98 percent of the original model quality while reducing memory requirements by roughly eight times. This means you can run a 70 billion parameter model on a single consumer GPU with 24 gigabytes of VRAM, something that was completely impractical just two years ago.

Next, mixture of experts architectures have been a game changer. Instead of activating every parameter for every token, MoE models like Mixtral route each token through only a subset of specialized expert networks. The result is that you get the quality of a much larger dense model while only using a fraction of the compute per forward pass. Mixtral 8x7B, for example, matches GPT-3.5 quality while activating only about 13 billion parameters per token out of its total 47 billion.

Knowledge distillation is another powerful approach. The idea is straightforward: train a smaller student model to replicate the behavior of a larger teacher model. Recent work has shown that carefully designed distillation pipelines can produce models that are ten times smaller yet retain 90 to 95 percent of the teacher's performance on standard benchmarks.

Finally, speculative decoding has emerged as a clever inference optimization. You use a small draft model to generate candidate tokens quickly, then verify them in parallel with the larger model. This can speed up generation by two to three times without any quality loss, because every accepted token is guaranteed to match what the large model would have produced.

These techniques are not mutually exclusive. The most efficient deployments combine quantization with mixture of experts and speculative decoding, achieving cost reductions of ten to fifty times compared to naive dense model inference. This is what's making it possible for smaller companies and research labs to deploy state-of-the-art language models without massive GPU clusters.
